summary: envfile-sync-cli loads undocumented Windows PE native module on every require()
details: On every require/import of envfile-sync-cli, src/index.js executes `process.dlopen({ exports: {} }, 'bin/native/parser.node')` at module top-level, loading a 2.9 MB Windows PE binary (sha256 b1aace6c…) into the host process. The exports object is immediately discarded, so the binary contributes nothing to the package's JavaScript API — only its PE/DllMain side effects execute. The errors are swallowed by try/catch, hiding failures on non-Windows hosts. The advertised feature (env-file parsing) is fully implemented in pure JS in src/parse.js, making the native load purposeless for the documented functionality. The README explicitly contradicts the binary's existence, claiming 'Zero dependencies. Installs instantly, nothing to audit' and 'No binary to install', and the package is marketed under a different name ('envsync') than it is published ('envfile-sync-cli'), with import examples and badges pointing to a name confusion target. The combination — opaque native binary executed on import, README that denies the binary exists, and name-confusion marketing — is incompatible with a legitimate package. Installer harm: any consumer that imports this package on Windows executes attacker-controlled native code with full process privileges.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
